content.text: What it is

Business sign-off and UAT acceptance criteria for agentic AI features are the formal conditions that must be satisfied before a new AI capability is released to users. Unlike traditional software release gates — which focus on functional correctness and performance — AI release gates must also address output quality thresholds, safety boundary verification, compliance artefacts, and explicit risk acceptance by responsible business stakeholders. Sign-off is not a formality: it is the moment at which accountable individuals confirm that the feature is fit for production use and accept the associated risks.

Acceptance criteria for agentic AI differ from standard user story acceptance criteria in two important ways. First, they reference statistical thresholds (pass rates, eval scores) rather than binary conditions, because AI behaviour is probabilistic. Second, they require sign-off from multiple stakeholders representing different accountability domains: product management owns functional and quality criteria, legal or compliance owns privacy and disclosure criteria, and engineering leads own technical reliability criteria.
Why it matters

Without explicit, written acceptance criteria and a structured sign-off process, AI features get released based on informal engineering consensus. This creates accountability gaps: if a released feature causes harm — financial, reputational, or user-facing — there is no record of who evaluated it against what criteria before it shipped. As AI systems become subject to regulation (EU AI Act, sector-specific AI governance requirements), documented sign-off with clear acceptance criteria becomes a legal and audit requirement, not just a quality practice.
Benefits
Creates a clear, documented record of who accepted the feature and on what evidence — essential for regulatory compliance
Forces explicit articulation of quality and safety thresholds before the UAT session, preventing post-hoc rationalization
Distributes accountability across appropriate stakeholders rather than concentrating it in engineering
Prevents releasing features that 'technically work' but do not meet business quality expectations
Provides a repeatable gate that can be compared across releases to track improvement over time
Trade-offs and limitations
Multi-stakeholder sign-off processes can slow releases if stakeholders are not available or responsive
Statistical thresholds (e.g., 95% functional pass rate) require enough UAT scenarios to be statistically meaningful — fewer than 20 scenarios per dimension produces unreliable rates
Acceptance thresholds set too high block legitimate releases; thresholds set too low allow sub-standard features through
Executive sign-off for major releases adds stakeholder overhead that must be scheduled in advance
Criteria must be updated when the feature scope changes — stale acceptance criteria can approve features against outdated requirements
How it works

The acceptance criteria framework covers four sign-off domains. UAT pass rates (owned by product management) require functional scenario pass rate at or above 95%, quality scenario pass rate at or above 80% at the 'Acceptable' threshold or better, and safety scenario pass rate at exactly 100% — any safety failure blocks release regardless of other scores. The eval suite gate (owned by the engineering lead) requires that the automated eval suite shows no regression versus the previous release's baseline score and that all new scenarios written for the current release pass.

Legal and compliance sign-off (where applicable) requires completion of a DPIA if the feature processes personal data in a new way, approval of the disclosure text that informs users they are interacting with AI, and confirmation that the feature's output does not constitute regulated advice (financial, legal, medical) without appropriate disclaimers. Executive sign-off is required for major releases — those introducing a new agent capability, a new data access scope, or a new user-facing interaction model — and requires confirmation that the business case metrics are achievable and that residual risks have been accepted.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Business Sign-Off Gate: four domains — UAT Pass Rate, Eval Suite, Legal/Compliance, Executive — with ownership and thresholds

content.text: The four domains have different owners and different trigger conditions. UAT pass rates and eval suite gates are required for every release. Legal/compliance and executive gates activate for releases that meet defined escalation criteria.
When to use it

Apply this sign-off framework to every release of an agentic AI feature. Calibrate which gates activate based on release type: patch releases fixing bugs may require only the eval suite gate; minor feature additions require UAT pass rates and eval suite; major releases introducing new agent capabilities or data scopes require all four gates. Document the release classification criteria in the team's release process runbook so engineers can determine which gates apply without asking the product manager each time.
How to implement
Define acceptance thresholds for each domain before the UAT session begins: functional pass rate, quality pass rate, safety pass rate, eval regression limit
Assign a named owner to each sign-off domain: product manager for UAT, engineering lead for eval, legal representative for compliance, executive sponsor for major releases
Create a sign-off checklist document that lists each gate, its threshold, the evidence required, and the owner
After the UAT session, populate the checklist with actual pass rates and attach the UAT session results
Route the populated checklist to each gate owner for review and signature (electronic or documented Slack approval)
Block the deployment pipeline until all required gate approvals are recorded in the checklist document
Archive the completed checklist with the release artefacts for compliance and audit purposes
Examples

# Release Sign-Off Checklist: AI Claims Advisor v2.3

## Release Classification: Minor — new capability (document upload)
## Required gates: UAT pass rates, Eval suite, Legal/compliance

---

## Gate 1: UAT Pass Rates (Owner: Sarah Chen, Product Manager)

| Dimension | Threshold | Actual | Status |
|-----------|-----------|--------|--------|
| Functional scenarios (n=24) | ≥ 95% | 96% (23/24) | PASS |
| Quality scenarios (n=12) | ≥ 80% Acceptable+ | 83% (10/12) | PASS |
| Safety scenarios (n=8) | 100% | 100% (8/8) | PASS |

**UAT session date:** 2026-07-10
**Evaluators:** Jane Smith (Claims Specialist), John Doe (Claims Manager)
**Sign-off:** [Sarah Chen] [2026-07-11]

---

## Gate 2: Eval Suite (Owner: Mike Torres, Engineering Lead)

| Check | Result | Status |
|-------|--------|--------|
| Regression vs baseline (v2.2) | +0.3% improvement | PASS |
| New scenarios (document upload, n=15) | 14/15 pass | PASS |
| P95 latency ≤ 2000ms | 1847ms | PASS |

**Sign-off:** [Mike Torres] [2026-07-12]

---

## Gate 3: Legal / Compliance (Owner: Emma Walsh, DPO)

| Check | Status |
|-------|--------|
| DPIA completed for document upload feature | COMPLETE (ref: DPIA-2026-047) |
| AI disclosure text approved | APPROVED (v3) |
| Document retention policy confirmed | CONFIRMED (30-day auto-delete) |

**Sign-off:** [Emma Walsh] [2026-07-13]

---

## RELEASE DECISION: APPROVED
**Target deployment:** 2026-07-15 08:00 UTC
Real-world example

A fintech company deploying an AI-powered loan eligibility advisor initially used informal sign-off: an engineer marked a Jira ticket 'Done' and deployed. After a regulatory review, the company discovered that the AI had been providing responses that constituted financial advice without the required disclaimers, and that there was no record of who had approved the feature or what checks had been run before launch. The regulator required a retrospective risk assessment and imposed a remediation timeline.

The company implemented a four-gate sign-off process with a documented checklist for every AI feature release. For the next release cycle, the legal gate review caught that the AI's output for borderline applicants included language that implied a decision had been made ('Based on your profile, you are likely eligible...') when the decision was still subject to manual review. The disclosure text was updated to clarify this before release. The sign-off checklist now serves as the primary audit evidence in regulatory reviews.
Common mistakes
Setting acceptance thresholds without consulting domain experts: a product manager should not set quality thresholds for medical or legal AI without input from those domains
Treating sign-off as a rubber-stamp process: if gate owners are not actually reviewing evidence, the sign-off provides false assurance
Using percentage thresholds without specifying the minimum sample size: a 100% pass rate on 3 scenarios is not statistically meaningful
Not versioning the acceptance criteria: criteria that change without documentation make it impossible to compare results across releases
Skipping the legal gate for features that 'only slightly' change how user data is processed: DPIA applicability is a legal judgment, not an engineering one
Not archiving sign-off records: in a regulatory audit, an unarchived sign-off is treated as a missing sign-off
Best practices
Define acceptance thresholds in the feature definition document before any code is written — this prevents threshold-setting being influenced by actual UAT results
Require a minimum of 20 scenarios per evaluation dimension to ensure pass rates are statistically meaningful
Set a calendar deadline for each gate: if the gate owner has not responded within 48 hours, escalate to their manager
Include a 'no objection' expiry: if a gate owner does not respond within the deadline and no concerns were raised, document this as a conditional approval with escalation
Review and update acceptance thresholds annually as the team's quality bar rises and the AI's capabilities mature
For safety scenarios, require a written statement from the product manager that all known failure modes have been tested and no new ones were discovered in UAT
Performance and scalability

The sign-off process adds 2–5 business days to the release cycle when all four gates are active. Reduce cycle time by running gate reviews in parallel rather than sequentially, sharing the UAT results with all gate owners simultaneously rather than waiting for each to complete before contacting the next. Pre-schedule sign-off time with legal and compliance stakeholders at the start of each sprint, so there is no queue waiting for their review at the end of the sprint. For high-velocity teams with weekly releases, implement a lightweight async sign-off process for minor releases using a structured Slack approval thread in place of a formal checklist document.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
Confluence / Notion
Sign-off checklist document
Version-controlled; supports inline approval comments
Jira
Release blocking ticket
Link checklist to the release ticket; block deployment until resolved
DocuSign / HelloSign
Electronic signature for formal sign-offs
Required for regulated industries where digital signatures are mandated
Slack approval bots
Lightweight async approval for minor releases
Slackbot approval threads can trigger CI deployment on approval
Linear
Combined sign-off and issue tracking
Supports multi-stakeholder review workflows
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Structured multi-gate sign-off (this topic)
Clear accountability; audit trail; statistical rigour
Slower; requires stakeholder availability
Engineering-only release approval
Fast; no dependencies
No business quality verification; accountability gap
Post-launch monitoring only
No release friction
Problems discovered after users are affected
Continuous deployment with feature flags
Incremental risk; rapid rollback
Requires sophisticated flag governance to match sign-off rigour
Security and reliability

Sign-off checklists containing UAT session results may include AI-generated output samples or evaluator comments that reference sensitive business data. Store completed checklists in a document management system with appropriate access controls — not in a public Confluence space. Sign-off records must be retained for the duration required by your regulatory context: typically 5–7 years for financial services, 3 years for most enterprise contexts. Implement a retention policy in the document management system that automatically flags records approaching deletion age for review.
Escalation criteria and conditional approval

Not all sign-off decisions are binary. When a UAT session produces results near the acceptance threshold — for example, a 93% functional pass rate against a 95% threshold — the team needs a structured escalation process rather than an automatic block. Conditional approval allows a gate owner to approve the release with documented conditions: a list of defects that must be fixed before the next release, or a monitoring requirement that will detect if the failure mode manifests in production.

Conditional approval must be bounded: it is not a mechanism for permanently waiving acceptance criteria. Each conditional approval must include a remediation sprint by which the condition will be resolved and a monitoring metric that will confirm resolution. If the remediation sprint passes without the condition being addressed, the feature returns to the release queue with the original blocking issue still open. Document the conditions, the responsible owner, and the resolution deadline in the sign-off checklist.
Allow conditional approval when results are within 5% of the threshold and all safety scenarios pass
Each condition must specify: the defect or gap, the responsible engineer, and the resolution sprint
Conditional approvals require explicit product manager sign-off that the risk is acceptable given production monitoring
Unresolved conditional approval conditions block the next minor or major release of the same feature
Audit conditional approvals quarterly: if a team is consistently using conditional approval, the acceptance thresholds may be miscalibrated
Acceptance criteria evolution over the feature lifecycle

Acceptance criteria for an AI feature should not be static. In the early stages of a feature's life — the first two or three release cycles — the team is learning what good looks like and thresholds may be deliberately lenient to allow the feature to reach users and collect real feedback. As the feature matures and the team accumulates data on what quality levels users actually need, thresholds should tighten. A quality pass rate threshold of 75% is reasonable at launch; after 12 months of production data, the same feature might warrant a 90% threshold.

Document threshold revisions with a rationale in the feature's quality history log. Include: the previous threshold, the new threshold, the data evidence for the change (user satisfaction scores, support ticket rates, UAT results trends), and the date of the revision. This log provides the audit trail for regulatory reviewers who want to understand how the team's acceptance standards evolved over time.
Review and update acceptance thresholds annually using production quality data (support tickets, user satisfaction scores, complaint rates)
Document threshold revisions with rationale in the feature's quality history log
Start with lenient thresholds at launch and tighten them as the team accumulates quality evidence
Never lower thresholds for safety scenarios: safety gates must be at 100% regardless of feature maturity
Include threshold revision history in the annual quality report to demonstrate continuous improvement to auditors
Sign-off for high-frequency release cadences

Teams releasing conversational AI features weekly or bi-weekly cannot afford a 5-day sign-off cycle with four synchronous gate approvals. High-cadence release programmes need a tiered sign-off model where lightweight gates apply to low-risk releases and the full four-gate process applies only to high-risk ones. Define release risk using three dimensions: scope (how much of the AI system changes), data access (does the release touch new data types or scopes), and user impact (how many users are affected and how directly).

A low-risk release — a prompt engineering adjustment that improves tone without changing capability — might require only the eval suite gate (automated) and a product manager thumbs-up in Slack. A high-risk release — a new agent capability with document access — requires all four gates in parallel with a maximum 72-hour window. Document the risk classification criteria so engineers can self-classify releases without requiring product manager input on every deployment.
Define release risk tiers: low (eval gate only), medium (eval + UAT pass rates), high (all four gates)
Classify releases by scope, data access, and user impact to determine the applicable tier
Publish the risk classification criteria so engineers can self-classify without bottlenecking on product managers
Limit the full four-gate process to high-risk releases (targeting fewer than 20% of all releases)
For low-risk releases, async Slack approval from the product manager is sufficient if the eval suite passes
Documenting known limitations at sign-off

Every AI feature has known limitations at the time of release — capabilities it cannot perform reliably, edge cases where output quality degrades, or scope restrictions by design. The sign-off process must include an explicit step where the product manager documents these known limitations and confirms they are acceptable given the intended use case and user population. This documentation serves three purposes: it prevents support teams from filing bugs against expected limitations, it informs the user documentation and disclosure text, and it creates an audit record that the team knowingly shipped with these limitations and judged them acceptable.
Include a 'Known Limitations' section in the sign-off checklist for every release
Product manager must explicitly confirm that each listed limitation is acceptable for the target user population
Known limitations feed the user documentation and any AI-specific disclosure requirements
If a limitation is discovered after sign-off, add it as a post-release finding and evaluate whether a hotfix is warranted
Review known limitations at the start of each release cycle to confirm which are still relevant and which have been resolved
Post-release sign-off review

Sign-off does not end at deployment. Schedule a post-release review 2–4 weeks after launch to compare production behaviour against the acceptance criteria that were evaluated in UAT. Did the functional pass rates in UAT predict actual user success rates? Did the safety scenarios cover the failure modes that actually appeared in production? If production metrics diverge significantly from UAT predictions, investigate whether the UAT scenario set was representative or whether the acceptance thresholds need recalibration. This feedback loop makes each successive release's sign-off process more predictive and reliable.
Schedule a post-release review 2–4 weeks after launch comparing production metrics to UAT predictions
Track prediction accuracy: does the UAT functional pass rate predict actual user success rate within 10%?
When production metrics diverge significantly from UAT results, investigate scenario representativeness
Add any production failure modes not covered by UAT scenarios to the next release's test set
Use post-release review findings to recalibrate acceptance thresholds for the next release cycle
Key takeaways
Define acceptance thresholds before UAT begins, not after — post-hoc threshold setting is rationalisation, not quality assurance
Assign named owners to each sign-off gate: product management for UAT, engineering for eval, legal for compliance, executive for major releases
Safety scenario pass rate must be 100% — any safety failure blocks release regardless of other scores
Archive signed-off checklists as compliance evidence with a minimum 3-year retention
Run gate reviews in parallel to keep the release cycle under 5 business days even with four active gates
Minimum 20 scenarios per evaluation dimension for pass rate statistics to be meaningful
